Why Third-Party Testing Matters for Supplements

The FDA does not approve dietary supplements before they hit shelves. That means the label on your protein powder or creatine is essentially self-reported by the manufacturer. Independent testing by organizations like NSF International, USP, and Informed-Sport is the only way to verify what is actually in the product.

Studies have found that up to 30% of supplements contain ingredients not listed on the label, including banned substances, heavy metals, or underdosed active ingredients. Third-party tested products have passed rigorous checks for label accuracy, contaminant levels, and manufacturing quality.

Our Trust Score combines certification status, label transparency, filler detection, and consumer ratings into a single 0–100 number so you can compare at a glance.

Trust Score formula: 40pts (third-party cert) + 20pts (rating) + 15pts (verified servings) + 15pts (open label) + 10pts (no fillers) = 100 max

Supplements by Category

We track five major supplement categories. Each has different quality signals to watch for.

🥛 Whey Protein

Perfect for: muscle recovery, meal replacement, and meeting daily protein goals. Look for whey isolate over concentrate for higher protein per scoop. Key red flags: proprietary blends, amino spiking, and maltodextrin filler.

Popular searches: grass fed whey protein, NSF certified whey, cleanest whey protein powder, whey isolate vs concentrate

⚡ Pre-Workout

Perfect for: energy, focus, and pump during training. Clinically dosed citrulline (6-8g), beta-alanine (3.2g), and caffeine (150-300mg) are the benchmarks. Avoid proprietary blends that hide under-dosed ingredients behind a total weight.

Popular searches: clean pre workout no artificial sweeteners, sucralose free pre workout, best third party tested pre workout

💪 Creatine

Perfect for: strength, power output, and cognitive function. Creatine monohydrate is the most studied form — fancy variants (HCl, buffered) offer no proven advantage. Best value: unflavored powder in bulk. Check for Creapure sourcing and third-party purity testing.

Popular searches: creatine monohydrate third party tested, cheapest creatine per serving, NSF certified creatine, creapure creatine

💧 Electrolytes

Perfect for: hydration during exercise, keto diets, and hot-weather performance. Key electrolytes: sodium (1000mg+), potassium, and magnesium. Watch for sugar-loaded formulas marketed as "hydration" — true electrolyte mixes have minimal or zero sugar.

Popular searches: electrolyte powder no sugar, LMNT alternative, clean electrolyte mix, best electrolytes for keto

💊 Multivitamins

Perfect for: filling nutritional gaps, especially on restricted diets. USP or NSF certification is critical here — multivitamin potency degrades over time, and independent testing confirms the labeled dose is still accurate at expiration. Avoid mega-doses above 100% DV for most nutrients.

Popular searches: USP verified multivitamin, best third party tested multivitamin, clean multivitamin no fillers

Find Supplements by Certification & Clean Label

Not sure what to look for? These cards explain each quality signal we track. Click any card to filter the tracker above instantly.

🛡️ NSF Certified

Independently verified by NSF International for label accuracy, contaminant screening, and banned substance testing. The gold standard for competitive athletes.

View NSF products →

🛡️ USP Verified

USP tests for potency, purity, and dissolution — confirming the labeled dose is accurate through expiration. Especially critical for multivitamins and minerals.

View USP products →

✅ Third-Party Tested

An independent lab — not the brand — verified what is in the bottle. Catches heavy metals, undeclared ingredients, and label inaccuracies that self-testing misses.

View third-party tested →

🏅 Informed-Sport

Every batch tested for 270+ WADA-banned substances. Required by many professional sports leagues. If you compete, this is the certification to look for.

View Informed-Sport →

🚫 Sucralose-Free

No sucralose (Splenda). These products use stevia, monk fruit, or no sweetener at all. Increasingly popular with consumers avoiding artificial sweeteners for gut health.

View sucralose-free →

🧪 No Artificial Ingredients

Free from artificial sweeteners, colors, and flavors. Clean formulas that rely on natural ingredients for taste and appearance.

View clean products →

🌾 Gluten-Free

Essential for celiac disease and gluten sensitivity. These products are formulated or tested to contain less than 20ppm gluten — the FDA threshold.

View gluten-free →

🌱 Vegan

No animal-derived ingredients. Plant-based protein sources, fermented creatine, and capsules made without gelatin. Growing 15% year-over-year in the supplement market.

View vegan products →

🌿 Non-GMO

Made without genetically modified organisms. Often paired with organic or clean-label positioning. Look for Non-GMO Project verified for the highest assurance.

View Non-GMO →

📋 Transparent Label

Every ingredient dose is listed — no proprietary blends hiding underdosed actives. You know exactly how much citrulline, creatine, or caffeine you are getting per scoop.

View transparent labels →

📊 Clinically Studied

Key ingredients dosed at levels shown effective in peer-reviewed research. Citrulline at 6-8g, beta-alanine at 3.2g, creatine at 5g — not pixie-dusted amounts.

View clinically dosed →

🐄 Grass-Fed

Whey sourced from grass-fed, pasture-raised cows. Higher in omega-3 fatty acids and CLA compared to conventional whey. A premium quality signal for protein buyers.

View grass-fed →

Supplement Certification Guide: NSF vs USP vs Informed-Sport

With over 80% of supplements never independently tested, knowing which certifications to trust is the single most important buying decision you can make. Here is what each certification actually tests — and what it does not.

🛡️ NSF Certified for Sport

NSF International tests every batch for 270+ banned substances, verifies label claims, and audits manufacturing facilities. It is the most comprehensive sports supplement certification available. Required by the NFL, MLB, PGA Tour, and the Canadian Centre for Ethics in Sport.

What it tests: Label accuracy, contaminant levels (heavy metals, pesticides), banned substance screening, GMP facility audit.

What it does not test: Efficacy — NSF confirms what is in the bottle, not whether the product works.

Brands with NSF: Thorne, Momentous, Klean Athlete, NOW Sports (select products). Look for the blue NSF Certified for Sport mark — not just "NSF" alone.

🛡️ USP Verified

The United States Pharmacopeia tests for potency, purity, and dissolution. This means the product contains what the label says, dissolves properly in your body, and is free from harmful contaminants. Especially important for vitamins and minerals where potency degrades over time.

What it tests: Active ingredient amounts match the label through expiration date, dissolution rate, absence of contaminants.

Best for: Multivitamins, vitamin D, fish oil, and minerals where dosing accuracy matters most.

Brands with USP: Nature Made (most products), Kirkland Signature (select), TheraNatal. USP Verified is a gold standard for non-sport supplements.

🏅 Informed-Sport vs Informed-Choice

Informed-Sport tests every production batch before release. Informed-Choice tests monthly but not every batch. For competitive athletes, Informed-Sport is the safer choice — it catches batch-level contamination that monthly testing misses.

Both programs screen for substances banned by WADA. Accepted by UFC, Premier League, World Rugby, and most Olympic federations.

⚠️ "Third-Party Tested" Without a Name

Many brands claim "third-party tested" without naming the lab. This is better than nothing — but far less reliable than NSF, USP, or Informed-Sport. A named, accredited certifier holds the brand accountable. An unnamed lab does not.

Rule of thumb: If the brand does not name the testing lab or link to a certificate, treat "third-party tested" as a marketing claim, not a guarantee.

Clean Label Supplement Guide: What "Free-From" Claims Actually Mean

Labels like "sucralose-free" and "no artificial sweeteners" drive real purchasing decisions — but not all claims are equally meaningful. Here is what to look for and what to ignore.

🚫 Sucralose-Free vs No Artificial Sweeteners

Sucralose-free means no Splenda specifically — the most common artificial sweetener in supplements. No artificial sweeteners is broader: it excludes sucralose, aspartame, acesulfame-K, and all synthetic sweeteners. These products typically use stevia, monk fruit, or erythritol instead.

Why people search for this: Emerging research links artificial sweeteners to gut microbiome disruption. Whether the science is settled or not, consumer demand is clear — "sucralose free pre workout" is an 8K/month search query.

Clean sweetener hierarchy: No sweetener > Monk fruit > Stevia > Erythritol > Sucralose > Aspartame. This tracker flags which sweetener each product uses.

📋 Transparent Label vs Proprietary Blend

A transparent label discloses the exact dose of every active ingredient. A proprietary blend lists ingredients but hides individual amounts behind a total weight — making it impossible to verify if key ingredients are dosed effectively.

Why it matters: A pre-workout with a "6g Energy Blend" could contain 5.5g of cheap maltodextrin and 500mg of citrulline. Without individual doses, you cannot know. Over 60% of pre-workouts still use proprietary blends.

🌾 Gluten-Free Supplements

The FDA requires products labeled "gluten-free" to contain less than 20 parts per million (ppm) of gluten. For people with celiac disease, even trace amounts can trigger immune responses. Most protein powders are naturally gluten-free, but cross-contamination during manufacturing is common — especially with flavored products that use additives.

🌱 Vegan Supplements

Vegan supplements exclude all animal-derived ingredients: whey (from milk), gelatin capsules (from bones), vitamin D3 from lanolin (sheep wool), and omega-3 from fish oil. Vegan alternatives include pea protein, algae-based omega-3, lichen-derived vitamin D3, and cellulose capsules.

⚗️ Common Fillers to Watch For

Not all ingredients on a supplement label are active. Common fillers include maltodextrin (cheap carb filler), magnesium stearate (flow agent), silicon dioxide (anti-caking), and titanium dioxide (whitening agent banned in the EU for food use). None of these contribute to the product's effectiveness.

Red flags: If maltodextrin or dextrose appears in the first 3 ingredients, the product is mostly filler. Check the "Fillers" column in our tracker to identify these products instantly.

Supplement FAQ

Answers to the most common questions about supplement quality, testing, and value.

What does third-party tested mean for supplements?

Third-party tested means an independent lab — not the supplement company — verified that the product contains what the label claims, is free from harmful contaminants like heavy metals and pesticides, and meets quality standards. The most respected certifiers are NSF International, USP, and Informed-Sport. Products without any third-party testing rely entirely on self-reported label claims.

What is a proprietary blend and why should I avoid it?

A proprietary blend lists ingredients without disclosing individual amounts — you only see the total weight of the blend. This means you cannot verify if citrulline is dosed at the clinically effective 6g or at a useless 500mg. Transparent labels show exact doses for every ingredient. Over 60% of pre-workouts use proprietary blends to hide under-dosing.

How is the Trust Score calculated?

Trust Score (0-100) weighs five factors: third-party certification (40 points), average consumer rating normalized to 20 points, verified serving count (15 points), open label vs proprietary blend (15 points), and absence of common fillers like maltodextrin or magnesium stearate (10 points). A score above 70 indicates a highly trustworthy product.

What is the cheapest way to buy creatine per serving?

Bulk unflavored creatine monohydrate powders offer the lowest price per serving, often under $0.15. Brands like BulkSupplements and Nutricost sell 1kg tubs with 200 servings for under $20. Always verify third-party testing — at this price point, purity matters more than flavor. Avoid creatine HCl or buffered creatine variants that charge 3-5x more with no proven advantage.

Is NSF certification better than USP for supplements?

Both are rigorous. NSF Certified for Sport is preferred by competitive athletes because it also screens for 270+ banned substances. USP Verified focuses on potency, purity, and dissolution. For general consumers, either certification means the product passed independent testing — both are far better than no certification at all.

Are sucralose-free pre-workouts less effective?

No. Sucralose is an artificial sweetener with zero impact on workout performance. Sucralose-free pre-workouts typically use stevia or monk fruit instead. What matters for effectiveness is the dose of active ingredients like citrulline (6-8g), beta-alanine (3.2g), and caffeine (150-300mg) — not which sweetener is used.

What is the difference between "third-party tested" and "lab tested"?

"Third-party tested" means an independent lab — separate from the brand — verified the product. "Lab tested" is vague and often means the brand tested its own product in-house. In-house testing has an obvious conflict of interest. Always look for a named certifier (NSF, USP, Informed-Sport) rather than a generic "lab tested" claim.

Why do some supplements cost 3x more per serving than others?

Price differences come from ingredient quality (grass-fed whey vs conventional), manufacturing standards (cGMP-certified facilities cost more to operate), third-party testing fees ($5,000-$25,000 per product per year for NSF), and marketing spend. Our Trust Score and price-per-serving columns help you find high-quality products that are not overpriced.

Can I trust supplements sold on Amazon?

Amazon is a marketplace — quality varies enormously. Some brands sell directly (first-party), others through third-party sellers who may sell expired, counterfeit, or improperly stored products. To reduce risk: buy directly from the brand's Amazon storefront, check for third-party certifications (NSF, USP), and verify the seller is "Ships from and sold by Amazon" or the brand itself.

What does cGMP certified mean for supplements?

cGMP (current Good Manufacturing Practices) means the facility follows FDA-mandated quality standards for identity, purity, strength, and composition. While all US supplement manufacturers are required to follow cGMP, third-party cGMP audits by NSF or NPA provide independent verification that the facility actually complies — not just claims to.

Are Amazon Warehouse supplement deals safe to buy?

Amazon Warehouse deals are returned or damaged-packaging items resold at a discount. For supplements, the risk is improper storage — heat exposure degrades protein and probiotics. If the seal is intact and expiration is 6+ months away, it is generally safe. Avoid warehouse deals on probiotics, fish oil, and any temperature-sensitive supplements.

How long do supplements last after the expiration date?

Most supplements retain potency for 6-12 months past expiration if stored properly (cool, dry, sealed). Exceptions: probiotics lose viability quickly, fish oil can go rancid, and liquid vitamins degrade faster than tablets. USP-verified products are tested to confirm labeled potency through the printed expiration date.

Why This Tracker Exists

The supplement industry is worth $60 billion but largely self-regulated. We built this tracker to help consumers cut through marketing claims and find products backed by real third-party testing, transparent labels, and fair pricing.
